description:
In development since 2014, the Critter Quiz was born when the founder became concussed and was forbidden to read or go on their computer. Naturally, they chose to research over 130 different animals to develop a personality quiz to give out to all their friends. It did, in fact, require some reading and going on the computer. 
 Flash forward to today, and the Critter Quiz may have fewer animals, but is more ambitious than ever.
banner1: By connecting quiz-takers to one of 32 endangered species, we hope to motivate them to get involved in environmental conservation.
methodology: The Critter Quiz relies on five categories to organize animals and link them to human traits. Many generalizations are made below for clarity, but if you want to learn more about our results, take the quiz and see for yourself!
po:
Whether an animal is predator or prey was the first factor we considered. Most predators have to be diligent go-getters that have to “close” on the kill. Most prey species are scavengers that must be flexible and adapt as situations become hostile. 
 If you receive a predator species on the quiz, such as the Great Hammerhead Shark or a Blakiston’s Fish Owl, you are more likely to be organized, hold a schedule, and thrive in structured environments. If you receive a prey animal, such as the Kakapo or Saola, you are likely more open-minded, spontaneous, and skilled at changing gears.
sbc:
Some animals have complex relationships, and others are complete loners. The quiz translates this to the quiz-taker’s decision making and willingness to trust. To what degree do you bear others in mind when making decisions? How do you form strong bonds? 
 If you receive a socially complex animal, such as a Rusty Patched bumblebee or African Forest Elephant, you pride yourself on your empathy, and base your decisions on your values. If you receive a more independent animal, such as a Snow Leopard or Chinese Alligator, you are a logical person, leaning towards analysis.
ebe:
When a new friend suddenly falls quiet, it can be hard to tell whether it's a sudden burst of nerves, or potential disinterest. To assess the way an animal carries itself in the world, the factors considered are whether it is territorial, friendly to other species, and flees when it encounters others. 
 If you receive a more extroverted animal, such as an Orca Whale or Tapanuli Orangutan, you are likely social, and enjoy meeting new people. If you receive a more introverted animal, such as the Wild Camel or Giant Panda, you prioritize time to yourself, and prefer quieter environments.
me:
Migration goes beyond travel for food or mating, and also factors in whether an animal is stationary throughout its life or nomadic. Migratory species see more of the world, and stationary species are more grounded, (often literally and figuratively.) 
 If you receive a migratory animal, such as the Migratory Monarch Butterfly or Green Sea Turtle, you value imagination, and are future oriented. If you receive a more stationary animal, such as Axolotl or Red Wolf, you value realism, and focus on facts, experiences, and concrete details.
fcc:
An animal’s confidence in its environment is the last factor the quiz considers. It can be determined by food chain status, but size of the creature, existence of threats, and extroversion also factors in, as it is often a reflection of how comfortable an animal feels in its environment. 
 If you receive a confident animal, such as a Lion or Cross River Gorilla, you are self-assured, and readily regulate your emotions. If you receive a less confident animal, such as a Northern Rockhopper Penguin or African Wild Dog, stress may occasionally get the best of you, and you may struggle with confidence.
dont_fit:
Rather than eliminate some fantastic species, we chose to factor in what these traits mean in a more subjective way, opposed to sticking to strict definitions, such as predator or prey. 
 A great example is the Whale Shark. While technically a predator, (it is a filter feeder of marine species,) in our system it is prey. This is due to the fact that it is a filter feeder, and is therefore more passive—a neutral force in its environment. In our thinking, its go-with-the-flow attitude aligns it with the Giant Panda rather than a Great White Shark.
influence: Our founder came upon these categories organically when the quiz was first created, but research in the later years was influenced by the Myers-Brigg Type Indicator, The Big Five, and the Enneagram. All sources can be found in our comprehensive works cited.
